test.command: bash -c 'NVIDIA_BASE="$(python3 -c "import sys,os;print(os.path.join(sys.prefix,\"lib\",f\"python{sys.version_info.major}.{sys.version_info.minor}\",\"site-packages\",\"nvidia\"))" 2>/dev/null||true)" && if [ -d "${NVIDIA_BASE:-}" ]; then for d in "$NVIDIA_BASE"/*/lib; do [ -d "$d" ] && LD_LIBRARY_PATH="$d:${LD_LIBRARY_PATH:-}"; done; export LD_LIBRARY_PATH; fi && clj -M:test'

typecheck.detail: Clojure is dynamically typed — no typecheck validator configured

suggestedGuidanceUpdates.suggestion: Add coding convention: Never use clojure.core/read-string for data parsing — always use clojure.edn/read-string. The core read-string can execute arbitrary code via #= reader macros, which is a security risk when reading untrusted or file-sourced data.
suggestedGuidanceUpdates.evidence: Feature misc-pipeline-polish fixed a security issue where pipeline/core.clj used clojure.core/read-string to parse canonical-records.edn. A regression test now scans src/ for bare read-string calls, but a documented convention would prevent future workers from introducing new instances.

suggestedGuidanceUpdates.suggestion: Add coding convention: Always use explicit (:require ...) in ns forms — never rely on transitive namespace loading. If a namespace uses functions from clojure.string, clojure.set, etc., it must require them directly.
suggestedGuidanceUpdates.evidence: Feature misc-pipeline-polish fixed verification/core.clj which relied on transitive loading of clojure.string and clojure.set. However, the test file for this fix (pipeline_polish_test.clj) itself uses clojure.string functions without an explicit require, demonstrating the pattern is easy to fall into without a documented convention.
